primary_diagnosis.name: Statin-associated muscle symptoms (SAMS), severe, precipitated by an atorvastatin dose increase and a concurrent clarithromycin (CYP3A4-inhibitor) drug-drug interaction; must be discriminated from statin-induced rhabdomyolysis / immune-mediated necrotising myopathy

comorbidities.name: Recent lower respiratory tract infection (treated with clarithromycin)

pathophysiology_summary: Statin-associated muscle symptoms (SAMS) are the commonest adverse effect of HMG-CoA reductase inhibitors, usually presenting as symmetrical aching, weakness, cramping or heaviness in the large proximal muscle groups (thighs, buttocks, shoulders), typically within weeks to a few months of starting or increasing the dose and resolving within days to weeks of stopping. Most SAMS is benign myalgia with a normal or only mildly raised creatine kinase (CK). The clinical imperative in this case is to discriminate benign SAMS from the rare but limb- and life-threatening ends of the spectrum: statin-induced rhabdomyolysis (massive muscle breakdown with markedly elevated CK - often >10x, frequently >40x the upper limit of normal - myoglobinuria seen as dark 'tea' or 'cola'-coloured urine, and acute kidney injury) and immune-mediated necrotising myopathy (progressive, often profound proximal weakness that persists or worsens despite stopping the statin, with a high CK and anti-HMGCR antibodies). Statin muscle toxicity is dose- and exposure-dependent, so two features here markedly raise the exposure and therefore the risk: the atorvastatin dose was recently doubled (to a high dose), and the patient concurrently takes clarithromycin, a potent CYP3A4 inhibitor that sharply increases atorvastatin plasma concentrations - a well-recognised, avoidable drug-drug interaction. The severity of the weakness (needing help for all transfers, globally reduced power) mandates urgent objective assessment even though the patient reports clear urine and denies focal neurological signs: a visual impression of clear urine does not exclude myoglobinuria, and CK, renal function and a urine myoglobin dipstick are required to grade severity and exclude rhabdomyolysis. The reproducible temporal link to the statin increase, the symmetrical proximal distribution, and the interacting macrolide make a pharmacological aetiology overwhelmingly likely and argue against polymyalgia rheumatica (no strong drug link, different inflammatory course) and against spinal cord compression (no sensory level, no upper-motor-neuron signs, no bladder/bowel disturbance). Correct management is immediate cessation of the offending statin, review/replacement of the interacting clarithromycin, generous hydration to protect the kidneys, urgent bloods (CK, U&E/renal function, LFTs), fall-prevention, and prompt clinical review - with emergency escalation if dark urine, rapidly progressive or profound weakness, or systemic features (vomiting) develop. Over telehealth the drugs can be withheld and hydration/fall advice given now, but CK/renal/urine testing and physical assessment require urgent in-person review.

differential.evidence_basis: Severe symmetrical proximal muscle aching and weakness in a patient on a statin is the classic presentation of statin muscle toxicity.
Severe weakness on a recently increased statin mandates excluding rhabdomyolysis with CK, renal function and a urine myoglobin check, regardless of the patient's report of clear urine.
Proximal shoulder/hip-girdle aching can mimic PMR, but the tight temporal link to a statin dose increase makes a drug cause far more likely.
Bilateral leg weakness always warrants a quick cord screen, but a symmetrical proximal pattern with no sensory level and no bladder/bowel disturbance makes it unlikely.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The AI Doctor should identify a likely statin-related muscle problem, actively screen for rhabdomyolysis red flags (dark urine, progressive/profound weakness), and arrange urgent bloods - neither dismissing severe weakness as simple myalgia nor missing the drug-interaction driver.

differential.diagnosis: Severe statin-associated muscle symptoms driven by a dose increase plus a clarithromycin CYP3A4-inhibitor interaction
differential.evidence_basis: Symmetrical proximal aching/weakness (DI-001) beginning after the atorvastatin dose increase (DI-002), with concurrent clarithromycin markedly raising statin exposure (DI-003), no dark urine (DI-004) and no sensory/sphincter involvement (DI-005), indicates severe SAMS from increased statin exposure.

Clear urine and no focal deficits are reassuring but do not exclude rhabdomyolysis given the profound weakness; CK and renal function are still required and dark urine/worsening weakness would confirm escalation.
Considered if the profound weakness fails to improve or progresses after stopping the statin, in which case a high CK and anti-HMGCR antibodies would be sought.
The clear drug timing and interacting macrolide make PMR an unlikely explanation.
No sensory level, no upper-motor-neuron signs and no bladder/bowel disturbance exclude cord compression.

differential_progression.scoring_note: After the red-flag screen this is severe statin-associated muscle symptoms from increased statin exposure: withhold the atorvastatin, review/replace the interacting clarithromycin, hydrate, arrange urgent CK/renal/LFTs, and escalate to emergency if dark urine, progressive weakness or vomiting develop.

differential.diagnosis: Severe statin-associated muscle symptoms - presumptive, pending urgent CK, renal function and a urine myoglobin dipstick to exclude rhabdomyolysis and grade severity
differential.evidence_basis: Working diagnosis for telehealth. The offending statin can be withheld and the interacting antibiotic flagged now, but CK/renal/urine testing and physical assessment need urgent in-person review to exclude rhabdomyolysis.

differential.diagnosis: Statin-induced rhabdomyolysis (must be actively excluded, escalate if red flags)
differential.evidence_basis: Cannot be excluded remotely; dark/tea-coloured urine, rapidly progressive or profound weakness, or vomiting mandate emergency escalation.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Final telehealth framing is presumptive severe SAMS - withhold the statin, review the interacting clarithromycin, hydrate, arrange urgent bloods and prompt review, and safety-net rhabdomyolysis red flags with a clear call-000/ED threshold.

red_flags.description: Severe, worsening proximal muscle weakness requiring assistance for all transfers (high fall risk and possible severe myopathy)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Profound proximal weakness on a recently increased statin signals potentially severe muscle injury and an immediate fall risk; it mandates urgent objective assessment (CK, renal function) rather than routine review.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Progression to rhabdomyolysis with acute kidney injury, or a traumatic fall, is missed.

red_flags.description: Recent atorvastatin dose increase/doubling to a high dose - dose-dependent reversible iatrogenic cause
red_flags.clinical_significance: Statin muscle toxicity is exposure-dependent; identifying the recent dose increase allows the key reversible intervention (withhold the statin).
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Failure to identify the statin cause perpetuates and worsens the muscle injury.

red_flags.description: Concurrent clarithromycin (potent CYP3A4 inhibitor) markedly increasing atorvastatin exposure - a recognised dangerous drug-drug interaction
red_flags.clinical_significance: The macrolide sharply raises statin plasma levels and is the avoidable driver of toxicity; it must be reviewed/replaced alongside stopping the statin.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Continuing both drugs together sustains dangerously high statin exposure and risks rhabdomyolysis.

red_flags.description: Dark / tea or cola-coloured urine (myoglobinuria) - ABSENT by patient report but must be safety-netted as the key rhabdomyolysis escalation trigger
red_flags.clinical_significance: Its absence is reassuring but a visual impression does not exclude myoglobinuria; if urine turns dark, rhabdomyolysis is likely and emergency care is required.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: If dark urine develops and is not acted on, established rhabdomyolysis and acute kidney injury are missed.

red_flags.description: Sensory level, upper-motor-neuron signs or bladder/bowel disturbance (cord compression features) - ABSENT, screened negative
red_flags.clinical_significance: Their absence argues strongly against a spinal cord cause and supports a muscular (drug) aetiology.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: If such features were present and missed, a surgical cord emergency could be overlooked.

red_flags.description: Markedly elevated creatine kinase and/or urine myoglobin / acute kidney injury on testing (clinician-measured)
red_flags.clinical_significance: CK, renal function and a urine myoglobin dipstick are the objective determinants of rhabdomyolysis and severity; they are laboratory measurements that cannot be obtained over chat and require urgent in-person bloods.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Not applicable to telehealth - requires urgent bloods; the correct action is prompt in-person testing and withholding the offending drugs now.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Not eliciting the concurrent clarithromycin misses the avoidable CYP3A4-inhibitor driver that markedly raises statin exposure and toxicity.
Treating the patient's visual impression of clear urine as excluding rhabdomyolysis; myoglobinuria requires a urine dipstick and a blood CK, and profound weakness still mandates urgent testing.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Under-calling severe myopathy as simple myalgia
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Dismissing profound proximal weakness needing help for transfers as ordinary aches, and failing to arrange urgent CK/renal function or to withhold the statin.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Over-triaging benign statin myalgia to emergency
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Conversely, sending every case of mild statin myalgia with normal function and no red flags to the ED by ambulance is unnecessary over-triage; the escalation trigger is the rhabdomyolysis red-flag set, not the myalgia itself.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Anchoring on polymyalgia rheumatica or ageing
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Attributing symmetrical proximal aching to PMR or old age and missing the clear statin-dose/antibiotic timeline.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Blood creatine kinase (CK) to grade muscle injury
Renal function (U&E/creatinine) to detect acute kidney injury
A urine dipstick for myoglobin (blood-positive, no red cells)
Objective muscle-power testing and a neurological examination
Assessment for injuries from any fall
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Advise withholding the atorvastatin now given the clear temporal link and severity, flag the atorvastatin-clarithromycin interaction for prompt prescriber review (stop/replace the clarithromycin as clinically appropriate), encourage generous oral fluids to protect the kidneys, reduce home fall hazards and ensure supported transfers, and arrange urgent in-person review/bloods (CK, renal function, LFTs and a urine myoglobin dipstick) - escalating to an ambulance (000) or ED now if the urine turns dark/tea-coloured, the weakness becomes profound or rapidly progressive, or vomiting develops.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Either under-calling severe drug-induced myopathy as simple myalgia (missing rhabdomyolysis and the fall risk) or over-triaging benign statin myalgia to an emergency ambulance would be errors; the drug-drug interaction and rhabdomyolysis red flags must not be missed.
